primer.markdown:
# Two knobs: who the agent is, and what it can do

A raw `messages.create` call gives Claude a user turn and nothing else. Two small fields in that call turn it into something worth calling an agent. The first is `system` — a string (or structured block) that sets the agent's identity, constraints, and tone *before* any user turn. The TypeScript GUIDE shows it on lines 184-189: pass `system: "You are a helpful TypeScript expert. Provide concise, practical answers."` and every reply now inherits that character. The `system` field is the only persona the agent ever has; the user cannot override it mid-conversation.

The second field is `tools`. A tool is not code — it is a *schema* Claude reads to decide whether and how to call a function you own. The GUIDE defines one on lines 319-354: a `get_weather` tool with a `name`, a `description`, and an `input_schema` (JSON Schema describing the shape of `location` and `unit`). You hand the array of tool schemas to `messages.create` and Claude, if it judges the tool relevant, responds with a `tool_use` content block containing a concrete `input` object.

The trick is that this module stops *one step short* of actually executing the tool. That closing step — running the function, returning a `tool_result`, looping — is the subject of Module 04. Here you only learn to declare the schema and watch Claude choose to call it. That half alone is the entire contract: description quality drives call quality, and schemas are the agent's only view into your code.
primer.key_insight: `system` is who the agent *is*. `tools` is what the agent can *choose to reach for*. Both are declared on the same `messages.create` call; neither exists without it.

visual.alt: A messages.create call visualized as a box with four input slots stacked vertically: model, max_tokens, system (highlighted in gold with the text 'You are a TypeScript expert'), tools (highlighted in gold, an array holding a card with name=get_weather, description, input_schema). A fifth slot messages holds the user turn. An arrow exits the box carrying a response whose content array contains a tool_use block with name=get_weather and input={location: 'London'}.
visual.alt_es: Una llamada a messages.create visualizada como una caja con cuatro slots de entrada apilados: model, max_tokens, system (resaltado en oro con el texto 'Eres un experto en TypeScript'), tools (resaltado en oro, un array con una tarjeta con name=get_weather, description, input_schema). Un quinto slot messages contiene el turno de usuario. Una flecha sale de la caja llevando una respuesta cuyo content array contiene un bloque tool_use con name=get_weather e input={location: 'London'}.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated tool schema, identifies the three required fields (`name`, `description`, `input_schema`), and can explain why `description` matters for the model's tool-selection decision.

questions.prompt: In typescript/GUIDE.md lines 319-354, each tool object has exactly three required top-level fields before the schema is complete. Which trio?
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md lines 321-323 (get_weather) and lines 341-343 (calculate) show the same three fields. `name` is the identifier Claude returns in `tool_use.name`; `description` drives whether Claude decides to call it; `input_schema` is the JSON Schema the returned `tool_use.input` must match.

questions.prompt: typescript/GUIDE.md shows `system` prompts on lines 184-189. What does the `system` field define?
options: The agent's persistent identity and constraints, applied before any user turn.
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md lines 184-198: `system: "You are a helpful TypeScript expert. Provide concise, practical answers."` fixes the agent's role. The user turn (line 188: `messages: [{ role: "user", content: userMessage }]`) runs under that system prompt. The user cannot override `system` mid-conversation.

questions.prompt: A tool's `input_schema` uses JSON Schema. The `get_weather` schema on GUIDE.md lines 324-338 lists `location` and `unit` under `properties`, but `required: ["location"]`. What does this mean for Claude's call?
options: Claude must always supply both `location` and `unit`.
Claude must supply `location`; `unit` is optional and may be omitted.
Claude must supply `unit`; `location` is derived automatically.
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md line 337: `required: ["location"]`. Only `location` is mandatory. `unit` (lines 331-335) is optional and constrained to the enum `["celsius", "fahrenheit"]` when present. JSON Schema's `required` list is the exact same contract as in any other JSON Schema consumer.
